content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 22, 2004

Lindsay Crane
Firm Administrator
The Gualco Group, Inc.
770 L Street, Suite 1440
Sacramento, CA 95814-3325

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-04-032a

Dear Ms. Crane:

This letter is in response to your request on behalf of The Gualco Group, Inc. for advice regarding the lobbying provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter in its entirety corrects and supersedes the prior letter to you (Crane A-04-032) which was issued on March 18, 2004.

QUESTION

Is The Gualco Group, Inc., a lobbying firm, correct in registering the Lake County Sanitation District as their new client and disclosing payments for lobbying as coming from the Lake County Sanitation District even though the contract is with, and payments for lobbying are received from, Criterion Planners Engineers?

CONCLUSION

Yes.

_________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-04-032
Page No. 2

FACTS²

The Gualco Group, Inc., a lobbying firm, has recently been employed to lobby on
behalf of Lake County Sanitation District ("District"). Specifically, The Gualco Group,
Inc. has been retained to attempt to secure additional general obligation bond funds for a
project in the District entitled the Full Circle project ("project"). The contract is between
The Gualco Group, Inc. and Criterion Planners/Engineers ("Criterion"), project managers
employed by the District to oversee the project. Payments to The Gualco Group, Inc. will
be made by Criterion. Although the contract will be with Criterion, The Gualco Group,
Inc. will be lobbying entirely on behalf of the interests of the District. If additional
general obligation bond funds are forthcoming, they will be paid to the District.

ANALYSIS

The Act regulates the activities of lobbyists, lobbying firms and lobbyist
employers. Specifically, Chapter 6 of the Act requires lobbyists, lobbying firms and
lobbying firms to register and file periodic reports disclosing money spent to influence
legislative or administrative action. (Sections 86100-86118.) The Gualco Group, Inc. is
a lobbying firm, meaning a business entity which "receives or becomes entitled to receive
any compensation....for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action on
behalf of any other person, and any partner, owner, officer, or employee of the business
entity is a lobbyist." (Section 82038.5.) As a lobbying firm, The Gualco Group, Inc. is
required to disclose both information about each lobbyist employer with whom it
contracts to provide services and all payments received in connection with lobbying
activity. (Sections 86104, 86106, 86107, and 86114.) Thus, for purposes of your
question, we must determine whether either Criterion or the District is a "lobbyist
employer" as defined in section 82039.5.

Section 82039.5 defines a lobbyist employer, in relevant part, as any person, other
than a lobbying firm, who:

"(a) Employs one or more lobbyists for economic consideration,
other than reimbursement for reasonable travel expenses, for the
purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action."

Using this definition, the District is a lobbyist employer. We reach this
conclusion because the District is providing compensation, albeit through Criterion, to
The Gualco Group, Inc. for the "purpose of influencing legislative or administrative
action" in the form of securing additional general obligation bond funds. Criterion's
hiring of The Gualco Group, Inc. as a lobbyist appears to be part of the professional
consulting services they provide when working on a project. Thus, Criterion does not
meet the definition of a lobbyist employer in the context of hiring The Gualco Group,
Inc. in that it is not employing or contracting with anyone to lobby on its behalf. (Section
82039.5.) When Criterion contracts with The Gualco Group, Inc. to provide lobbying
services for the District, Criterion is an intermediary for the delivery of compensation

² Additional facts were provided in both telephone calls and e-mails.

File No. A-04-032
Page No. 3

from the District to The Gualco Group, Inc. (See Elsesser Advice Letter, No. I-98-255.)
When registering the new client, The Gualco Group, Inc. will disclose the District and
when reporting payments, the payments will be disclosed as coming from the District.

In addition, regulation 18613(a) states that a lobbying firm must disclose the
following:

"(1) The full name, address and telephone number of the person or entity
making the payment;
(2) If the payment was made on behalf of another person, the full name,
address and telephone number of the person on whose behalf the payment
was made . . . ."

Therefore, when payments are received from Criterion Planners/Engineers on
behalf of the Lake County Sanitation District, both entities must be disclosed on the
firm's quarterly reports.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Adrianne Korchmaros
Political Reform Consultant
